data.title: GSK in agreement with Rgenta to develop RNA-targeted small molecules
data.description:
• Privately held Rgenta Therapeutics has signed an agreement with GSK (NYSE:GSK) for multi-target strategic research to discover RNA-targeted small molecule splice modulators oncology and other disease areas.
  • Rgenta will use its proprietary discovery platform to develop oral RNA-targeting small molecule splice modulators against multiple targets selected by GSK.
  • Terms call for Rgenta to receive up to $46M in upfront and pre-option milestone payments and up to ~$500M per target in milestone payments as well as tiered royalties and a future equity investment.
  • GSK can also extend the agreement to include additional targets. If GSK exercises any of its options, it will be responsible for development and commercialization of any drug candidates.
  • Rgenta's lead candidate, RGT-61159 is an oral drug designed to modulate splicing of the transcription factor MYB, a mutated gene know to cause cancer. It is in phase 1a/b for potential use in adenoid cystic carcinoma, colorectal cancer, acute myeloid leukemia, and other solid tumors.

data.title: Shell to slow investments in offshore wind, split power business - Reuters
data.description:
Shell (NYSE:SHEL) is stepping back from new offshore wind investments and will split its power division following an extensive review of the business under CEO Wael Sawan's drive to boost profits, Reuters reported Wednesday.

Shell Energy (SHEL), which includes renewables, power generation and supply to customers, will be split into two separate power generation and trading units, a company spokesperson said.

"While we will not lead new offshore wind developments, we remain interested in offtakes where commercial terms are acceptable and are cautiously open to equity positions, if there is a compelling investment case," the company said.

The changes are part of a company-wide review launched in 2023 aimed at reducing costs as Sawan focuses on the highest-return business, which often has meant reducing spending on low-carbon and renewables activities and raising the focus on oil, gas and biofuels.

data.title: Apple, Baidu's AI team up in China see hurdles - report
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and Chinese company Baidu (BIDU) are collaborating to add AI features to iPhones sold in China, but the two companies are facing some difficulties, The Information reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

Engineers from Apple and Baidu are working to use Baidu's large language models, or LLMs, so they work well for iPhone users. However, the models have struggled to understand prompts and give correct responses to common scenarios posed by iPhone users, the report added.

The companies have also clashed over the use of data of iPhone users to train and improve the models. Baidu intends to save and analyze the data it receives from iPhone users making AI-related queries, but Apple's privacy policies prohibit the collection of such data, the report noted.

Last month, it was reported that Apple has been carrying out discussions with Chinese tech companies to help power Apple Intelligence in the country. A top Chinese official had noted that foreign companies would see a long approval process unless they partner with local companies. In October, the iPhone maker had started rolling out initial features of the AI tool in the U.S. for iPhones and other devices.

Apple is paying Baidu a fee for its AI models and has also pledged to cover the computing costs to retrain and fine-tune the models to meet the U.S. company's requirements. The agreement makes Apple one of the biggest customers for the Chinese internet search giant's AI models in terms of revenue, the report added.

In contrast, Apple is not compensating OpenAI for the additional costs incurred from handling the large inflow of iPhone users on its servers, and OpenAI is not paying Apple to be on its iPhones, the news agency previously reported.

Baidu is using its AI model Ernie 4.0 as the foundation for Apple's underlying generative AI services on devices such as the iPhone, Mac and iPad. The companies are teaming up on both large models that will run in the cloud and smaller models which will run on devices like the iPhone, according to the report.

Baidu's main challenge is providing more-relevant responses to the questions iPhone users may ask. For example, an iPhone user could ask Apple Intelligence in China to recommend a restaurant. Baidu's AI model may give an answer based only on data used to train its model, but Apple wants to give a more personalized answer based on a user's iPhone usage. This could include what apps a user has, their message history and other activities. However, this would require Baidu to do much more development work to make it happen, the report noted.

data.title: Trump nominates ShiftFour CEO for NASA Administrator
data.description:
President-elect Donald Trump nominated Shift4 Payments (NYSE:FOUR) CEO Jared Isaacman, who's also an astronaut, to lead the National Aeronautics and Space Administration.

Shift4 (NYSE:FOUR) stock dropped 4.3% in late morning trading on Wenesday.

"Jared will drive NASA's mission of discovery and inspiration, paving the way for groundbreaking achievements in Space science, technology, and exploration," Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social.

Isaacman also co-founded and was CEO of defense aerospace company Draken International for more than 10 years, Trump noted.

The executive was commander of Inspiration4, a private spaceflight that used SpaceX's Crew Dragon Resilience, which flew Sept. 16-18, 2021. He also commanded Polaris Dawn, another private spaceflight, in 2024, becoming the first private citizen to perform a spacewalk.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump names Peter Navarro as senior trade adviser
data.description:
President-elect Donald Trump named Peter Navarro to be senior counselor for trade and manufacturing in his second term as president.

Navarro also advised Trump on trade and manufacturing policy during his first administration. "During my First Term, few were more effective or tenacious than Peter in enforcing my two sacred rules, Buy American, Hire American," Trump said in a post on Truth Social. "He helped me renegotiate unfair Trade Deals like NAFTA and the Korea-U.S. Free Trade Agreement (KORUS), and moved every one of my Tariff and Trade actions FAST..."

Navarro served as White House director of trade policy, National Defense Production Act Policy Coordinator during the pandemic, deputy assistant to the president, and director of the short-lived White House National Trade Council at various times during Trump's first term.

data.title: Canada mining group urges trade cooperation with U.S. following China curbs
data.description:
China's decision to curb shipments of gallium, germanium and other key metals to the U.S. underscores the need for trade cooperation between mineral-rich Canada and the U.S., the Mining Association of Canada said Wednesday.

The statement follows President-elect Trump's threat to slap 25% tariffs on all goods coming from Canada and Mexico unless the countries each do more to stem migration and fentanyl.

"Imposing tariffs on Canadian mineral and metal exports to the U.S. would run counter to the shared goals of secure and reliable supply chains, [and] such measures risk disrupting the essential flow of these resources, undermining the competitiveness of North American industries," the association said.

More than half of Canada's mineral exports, totaling more than C$80B (~US$56.9B), were destined for the U.S. in 2022, according to the group, whose members include Barrick Gold (GOLD), Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Cameco (CCJ).

Meanwhile, Teck Resources (NYSE:TECK) said it is considering ways to increase production of germanium following China's move to ban shipments of the rare metal to the U.S.

Teck (TECK) is a primary supplier of germanium to the U.S. outside of China; the metal is a byproduct of the refining of zinc produced from Teck's Red Dog mine in Alaska, and the company's Trail smelter in British Columbia is one of the world's largest integrated germanium producers.

data.title: ACM Research cut to Hold at Needham on units being added to U.S. Entity List
data.description:
Needham downgraded ACM Research (NASDAQ:ACMR) to Hold from Buy, citing several ACM subsidiaries being added to the U.S. Entity List.

Shares of ACM were up about 1% on Wednesday.

Analysts led by Charles Shi noted that after the addition of ACM Shanghai and ACM Korea to the U.S. Entity List, they were downgrading ACMR to Hold. While the stock has pulled back, they think uncertainty around ACM's ability to source components to sustain the business, and the business relationship between ACM, a U.S. entity, and its banned subsidiaries in China and Korea, will remain an overhang for the stock, according to the analysts.

The analysts added that nearly all of ACM's business is within China, the Entity Listing, at least on the first order, should not affect the vast majority of its demand.

While the Entity List only regulates exports, ACM's U.S. customers, such as Intel (INTC) and European customers, such as Infineon, in theory, could continue to do business with ACM but might become more cautious to maintain ties, the analysts noted. Still, the financial impact in such a worst-case scenario will be immaterial, they added.

The analysts said that on the supply side, they think ACM still relies on the outside world, including the U.S., to source certain components. They think the company will struggle to maintain the relationship with its U.S. suppliers. Relationships with non-U.S., non-China suppliers may also be impacted if certain foreign direct product, or FDP, rules apply.

Overall, the analysts believe that the Entity Listing could affect ACM's ability to maintain supply even if demand is unaffected.

In addition, Shi and his team said that ACM, as a U.S. entity, in theory, is not banned from having business dealings with ACM units that are on the Entity List. However, the Entity Listing does create uncertainty around ACM as a parent company to ACM Shanghai and other sanctioned units. After all, ACM has no operating units of its own and must operate through ACM Shanghai and others, according to the analysts.

The analysts think this situation has created another layer of uncertainty for ACM, not only regarding the demand/supply issues but also for ACM shareholders.

data.title: Teva presents positive data from Ajovy pediatric study
data.description:
Teva (NYSE:TEVA) presented positive data from a Phase 3 study of its drug Ajovy in the prevention of episodic migraine in children.

The Israeli drugmaker said the study showed Ajovy significantly reduced the monthly migraine days and monthly headache days versus placebo in children aged six to 17 over a 12-week period.

Teva said the efficacy results were consistent with those from Phase 3 and real-world evidence studies in adults, with no new safety signals observed, according to a statement.

The data was presented at the European Headache Congress in the Netherlands.

Ajovy is already approved for the prevention of migraine in adults.

data.title: Chipotle's 20% price hike comes from a 'point of strength' - analyst
data.description:
To offset the higher price of ingredients, Chipotle Mexican Grill (NYSE:CMG) has raised their menu prices for the first time in a year, the company confirmed on Wednesday. The announcement drove shares higher, reaching a 5-month high in early trading even as the move was hinted at by the Wall Street Journal in late November.

“For the first time in over a year, we have taken a modest price increase of approximately 2% nationally to offset inflation,” the company’s chief corporate affairs officer said in an email.

“We think Chipotle is taking the menu price from a point of strength,” says Truist analyst Jake Bartlett, as restaurant traffic was strong in November and with no clear red flags with operating cost inflation.

With a menu price hike, Bartlett raised his price target for Chipotle by $2, and his EPS estimate for Q4 and FY24 by a penny to $0.26 and $1.13, respectively.

At the same time, Bartlett lowered his estimates for cost of goods sold (“COGS”) as the spot market prices of most of Chipotle’s food commodities are down, including chicken, beef, and dairy. The one notable exception is the escalating price for avocados, which has gone up 19% due to a brief import ban from Mexico compounded by poor yields in Mexico and Peru.

Truist maintained its Buy rating on Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) with a price target that implies a 21% upside from Tuesday’s close.
